era_context: The early 13th century was marked by the Crusader presence in the Levant, the consolidation of Islamic dynasties, the rise of Mongol power on the steppe, and the flowering of medieval European culture. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controlled northern China while the Song Dynasty held the south. The year 1206 was particularly significant for the proclamation of Temüjin as Genghis Khan, an event that would reshape Eurasian history over the following decades.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal contemporary documentation)
Interior of the Americas (limited written records)

events.title: Temüjin Proclaimed Genghis Khan at Kurultai
events.description: In 1206, Temüjin was proclaimed Genghis Khan ('universal ruler') at a kurultai (assembly) of Mongol tribes on the Onon River. This event marked the formal unification of the Mongol confederation and the beginning of Temüjin's transformation into the historical figure known as Genghis Khan. By 1206, Temüjin had defeated most major rival tribes (Tatars, Kereit, Jamukha's confederation) and established a centralized military hierarchy based on merit rather than birth. The kurultai of 1206 formalized this consolidation and gave Temüjin the title and authority to rule the unified Mongol confederation. This event was revolutionary in steppe politics, replacing tribal fragmentation with centralized hierarchy. The proclamation of Genghis Khan in 1206 set the stage for the Mongol conquests that would dominate the 13th century and reshape Eurasian history.

sources.name: Secret History of the Mongols (Mongol Yuan shi, compiled 13th century, based on oral tradition)
Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles, early 14th century)

events.certainty_note: The kurultai where Temüjin was proclaimed Genghis Khan is traditionally dated to 1206, and this date is accepted by modern scholarship. However, Mongol chronology is uncertain; some sources vary by ±5 years. The Secret History is the most detailed source but was compiled long after events.
cross_references: Led to Mongol invasions of Jin Dynasty (1205–1215)
Led to Mongol conquests of Central Asia and beyond (1206–1368)

events.title: Ayyubid Empire: Al-Adil I Consolidates Power
events.description: In 1206, Al-Adil I (Saif al-Din Abu Bakr Ahmad, r. 1200–1218) continued to consolidate Ayyubid power following the death of Saladin in 1193. By 1206, Al-Adil had secured control over Egypt, Syria, and parts of Mesopotamia, establishing a stable and prosperous empire. He was known for his administrative reforms, military organization, and diplomatic acumen. His reign represented a period of relative peace and cultural flourishing in the Islamic world, though it was punctuated by conflicts with the Crusader states and internal Ayyubid rivalries. Al-Adil's consolidation of power ensured the Ayyubid Empire's dominance in the eastern Mediterranean for another decade.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh (The Complete History, written early 13th century)
Baha al-Din Ibn Shaddad, Al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya (The Rare and Excellent Histories, written early 13th century)

events.certainty_note: Al-Adil I's reign and major policies are well-attested in Islamic sources. The specific year 1206 does not mark a major event but rather a point within his consolidation period.

events.title: Fourth Crusade: Latin Empire Consolidates Control
events.description: In 1206, the Latin Empire, established by the Fourth Crusade in 1204, was consolidating its control over Constantinople and surrounding territories. Emperor Henry of Flanders (r. 1206–1216) had recently assumed power and was working to stabilize the Latin occupation. However, the Latin Empire faced significant challenges: Byzantine successor states (the Empire of Nicaea, the Despotate of Epirus, and the Empire of Trebizond) competed for legitimacy and territory, and local resistance to Latin rule was growing. The Latin Empire's control was fragile and would eventually collapse in 1261 when the Byzantines reconquered Constantinople. In 1206, the Latin Empire was at its territorial zenith, though internal instability was already evident.
key_figures: Theodore I Laskaris (Emperor of Nicaea, rival claimant)
Pope Innocent III (papal overlord of the Latin Empire)

sources.name: Robert of Clari, La Conquête de Constantinople
Geoffrey of Villehardouin, Conquest of Constantinople

events.certainty_note: The Fourth Crusade and establishment of the Latin Empire in 1204 are well-documented. The state of the Latin Empire in 1206 is less detailed but can be inferred from contemporary sources.
cross_references: Follows from 1204-001 (Fourth Crusade sack of Constantinople)

events.title: Song Dynasty: Southern Song Consolidation Under Emperor Ningzong
events.description: In 1206, the Southern Song Dynasty (1127–1279) was in a period of cultural and economic flourishing under Emperor Ningzong (r. 1194–1224). The dynasty controlled southern China and was known for its achievements in printing, ceramics, painting, and philosophy. By 1206, the Southern Song was at the height of its economic power, with extensive trade networks and sophisticated administration. However, the dynasty faced long-term military pressure from the Jin Dynasty to the north. The Southern Song's economic prosperity was not matched by military strength, a disparity that would eventually contribute to the dynasty's vulnerability to the Mongol invasions.

sources.name: Song shi (History of the Song Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: The Southern Song Dynasty's existence and chronology are well-attested in Chinese sources. The year 1206 falls within Emperor Ningzong's reign.

events.title: Jin Dynasty: Jurchen Rule in Northern China
events.description: In 1206, the Jin Dynasty (1115–1234), ruled by the Jurchen people, controlled northern China under Emperor Zhangzong (r. 1190–1208). The dynasty maintained a sophisticated bureaucracy based on Chinese models, though Jurchen military traditions remained important. By 1206, the Jin Dynasty was facing increasing pressure from the Mongol confederation to the north, though these threats would not become acute until the 1210s. The Jin Dynasty's control of northern China represented a significant non-Han power in East Asia, and its eventual fall to the Mongols would reshape the political landscape of Asia.

sources.name: Jin shi (History of the Jin Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: The Jin Dynasty's existence and basic chronology are well-attested in Chinese sources. The year 1206 falls within Emperor Zhangzong's reign.

events.title: Khmer Empire: Jayavarman VII's Building Program at Zenith
events.description: In 1206, Jayavarman VII (r. 1181–c. 1220) continued his reign as the most prolific builder in Khmer history. By this date, the construction of Angkor Thom (the new capital, begun c. 1190) and the Bayon temple (begun c. 1190s) was well advanced. Jayavarman VII's building program was unprecedented in scale and represented a massive commitment of resources. The program included not only religious monuments but also infrastructure for public health and education, reflecting a sophisticated understanding of state power and legitimacy. By 1206, the Khmer Empire was at its territorial and cultural zenith, though the strain of Jayavarman VII's building program may have contributed to later decline.

sources.name: Inscriptions on Khmer temples and monuments
Archaeological evidence from Angkor Thom and the Bayon

events.certainty_note: Jayavarman VII's reign and building projects are well-attested through inscriptions and archaeology. The specific year 1206 cannot be pinpointed for individual construction phases.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 1206 is traditionally dated for Genghis Khan's proclamation, and this date is accepted by modern scholarship, though Mongol chronology remains uncertain (±5 years). Some sources dispute the exact significance of the kurultai; modern scholarship suggests that Temüjin's consolidation was more gradual than the traditional account implies. The Fourth Crusade's establishment of the Latin Empire in 1204 is well-documented, but the stability of the Latin Empire in 1206 is less detailed in sources. Some sources dispute the exact nature of Jayavarman VII's building program; modern scholarship suggests that the strain on resources may have been less severe than traditional accounts indicate.
historiographic_note: The year 1206 is moderately well-documented in European sources (crusade chronicles, papal correspondence) but less well-attested in Asian sources. Mongol history relies heavily on later compilations (Secret History, Rashid al-Din) rather than contemporary records. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on political and military events; economic and social developments are underrepresented. Khmer history depends on inscriptions and archaeology, which provide limited chronological precision. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite perspectives; peasant and urban perspectives are largely absent. The year 1206 is significant primarily for the Mongol unification; other regions experienced relatively quiet years.

graph_edges.note: Genghis Khan's proclamation in 1206 led to Mongol invasions of the Jin Dynasty beginning in 1205–1215
Genghis Khan's unification of the Mongol confederation in 1206 led to the establishment of the Mongol Empire, which lasted until 1368
The Latin Empire established in 1204 and consolidated in 1206 was eventually reconquered by the Byzantines in 1261
The Southern Song Dynasty's consolidation in 1206 was eventually disrupted by Mongol conquest in 1279
